segments.s137-g25: This noun phrase names the agents: سواران ترکان = 'the horsemen of the Turks' with ترکان in the genitive/attributive role. تنی هفت و هشت is a poetic numeric phrase 'some seven and eight' (i.e. seven or eight) placed after the head noun as a quantifier; و here is the coordinating 'and' inside the numeric expression.
segments.s137-g26: This is a short clause: بران دشت نخچیرگه = 'on that plain, the hunting-ground' with بران (بر + آن) 'on/over that' locating دشت نخچیرگه. برگذشت is a past tense verb 'passed by/passed over,' agreeing with the plural subject earlier.
segments.s137-g27: یکی اسپ دیدند = 'they saw one/a certain horse.' یکی functions as an indefinite numeral/indefinite ('one' / 'a certain'), اسپ is the direct object, and دیدند is the past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they saw.' در مرغزار is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the meadow' describing where the sighting occurred.
segments.s137-g28: بگشتند is the past plural of برگشتن here in the sense 'they turned/they went around'; گرد لب جویبار is an adverbial phrase 'around/about the bank (لب) of the stream (جویبار),' with گرد meaning 'around/about' and لب 'brink/bank.' Word order places the verb before its locative expression.
segments.s137-g29: چو = 'when.' بر دشت = 'on the plain' (بر + noun). مر is an archaic first‑person possessive/accusative form here referring to the speaker's ('my') steed; رخش را has the object marker را attached to mark Rakhsh as definite, and یافتند is the past plural 'they found.' The clause is temporal ('when they found...').
segments.s137-g30: سوی introduces direction/purpose 'toward(s)'; بند کردنش is a light-noun + کردن construction 'to bind/tie him' where ش is the 3rd‑person pronominal clitic 'his/him.' بشتافتند is the past plural 'they hurried/rushed.' Together the clause means 'they hurried toward binding him.'
segments.s137-g31: گرفتند و بردند are two coordinated past verbs 'they seized and they carried/took.' پویان here functions adverbially 'swiftly/actively' (a poetic adverb) modifying بردند. به شهر is the prepositional phrase 'to the city' marking destination.
segments.s137-g32: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'immediately/just/then'), هر یک 'each one,' از رخش is an ablative phrase 'from his steed,' and جستند is the past plural 'they leapt/jumped.' بهر at the end is the preposition 'for/to' that links forward to the following line (the clause is purpose/destination-oriented and continues).
segments.s137-g1: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source phrase 'ز گفتار دهقان' = 'from the speech of the dehqān'. یکی داستان places the indefinite modifier 'یکی' before the noun 'داستان' in the classical word order, meaning 'a certain tale' or simply 'a tale' that will be related.
segments.s137-g2: بپیوندم is a first‑person verb meaning 'I attach/relate' (classical prefix ب־ + verb stem); it takes the preceding 'یکی داستان' as its object. از گفته باستان is a source phrase 'from the ancient report/what is said of old' specifying where the tale comes from.
segments.s137-g3: ز موبد marks the source 'from the mobed'. برین گونه is the classical compound 'بر این گونه' = 'in this manner/thus' (here written compactly), with بر used as a prepositional/forming element and ین as the orthographic variant of 'این'. بر داشت یاد is a verbal phrase ('برداشتِ یاد') where بر functions as the separable preverb 'up/away' and داشت is the past auxiliary/verb, so the whole means 'he took up/recorded a remembrance' or simply 'he related this recollection.'
segments.s137-g4: که is the subordinating particle 'that' (introducing the reported content). The clause gives the subject and time: 'رستم' (Rostam) + 'یکی روز' ('one day' / 'a certain day') + 'از بامداد' ('from dawn' = 'in the morning'). Word order is typical: the temporal phrase follows the indefinite 'یکی روز'.
segments.s137-g5: غمی بد is a noun + adjective in inverted (poetic) order meaning 'a grievous sorrow'. دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش). ساز ... کرد is an idiom 'to make into / to set as' where ساز here is 'device/means' metaphorically; نخچیر is the object 'prey/hunting', so the clause means 'a grievous sorrow made his heart [turn] to hunting.' کرد is the past tense 'made/did.'
segments.s137-g6: کمر بست is a common phrase 'he girded his waist' (کمر = waist, بست = bound/tied). The second clause 'ترکش پر از تیر کرد' has ترکش (here split as ترک + ش) = 'his quiver', پر از تیر = 'full of arrows' (پر = 'full (of)'), and کرد = 'he made/did' — so 'he filled his quiver with arrows.'
segments.s137-g7: سوی مرز توران is a directional noun phrase 'toward the border of Turan' (سوی = toward, مرز = border, توران = Turan). چو بنهاد روی is a temporal/conditional clause 'when he set his face' (چو = 'when/as', بنهاد = 'he set/placed', روی = 'face'). The clause as a whole gives the time/manner of his movement.
segments.s137-g8: چو شیر introduces a simile 'like a lion'. The following compounds 'دژآگاه نخچیرجوی' are epithets: دژآگاه (watchful/fortress‑vigilant) and نخچیرجوی ('seeker of prey' = hunter), describing his manner as fierce and alert. The overall phrase compares his bearing to that of a lion, alert and pursuing game.
segments.s137-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when" introducing a subordinate time clause; نزدیکی ("nearness/vicinity") governs the genitive مرز توران ("the border of Turan"). رسید is the simple past 3rd-person singular "reached/came," with its subject omitted and supplied by context.
segments.s137-g10: بیابان is the noun being perceived ("the countryside/plain"); سراسر is an adverbial/emphatic modifier meaning "entire/throughout," and پر از is the adjectival phrase "full of" with از marking the thing filled. دید is the past tense verb "saw/beheld," so the whole phrase means "he saw the plain entirely full of graves."
segments.s137-g11: برافروخت is an intransitive past (3rd sg) meaning "flushed/was kindled" (poetic). چون introduces a simile "like," so گل رخ تاج‌بخش reads as a poetic epithet "the crown-giver's face like a flower" (i.e., his face bloomed/colored like a flower). The subject again is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s137-g12: بخندید is past 3rd sg "he laughed." وز here is an archaic connective (not the literal "wind/weight") linking clauses; جای ("place") combined with برکند (past of "uproot/dislodge") and رخش ("his steed") yields "and (then) his steed was torn/uprooted from its place." Word order places the verb before its object in this elevated/style-conscious line.
segments.s137-g13: به functions here as an instrumental/agentive preposition ("with/by") and is repeated for rhythmic emphasis: "with arrow and bow and with mace and lasso." The nouns are simply coordinated by و, listing the weapons/tools used.
segments.s137-g14: بیفگند is a past tense verb (classical form of افکندن/انداختن) meaning "threw/cast"; بر دشت marks the target "upon the plain," and نخچیر چند means "a few head(s) of game/prey." The subject is omitted (understood from prior context or plural agents), so the clause reads "they cast onto the plain a few (heads of) game."
segments.s137-g15: ز is the poetic preposition "from," introducing materials; the list (خاشاک وز خار و شاخ درخت) enumerates fuel: "from chaff, and from thorn and branch of the tree." وز here is used as a poetic coordinate (not the literal senses given in the lexicon), and the internal coordination places درخت ("tree") as the possessor of شاخ ("branch/bough").
segments.s137-g16: یکی ("one/a certain") qualifies آتشی ("a fire"); برفروزید is past tense "kindled/lit up" (poetic form), and سخت here is an adverbial modifier meaning "strongly/violently." Together: "a single strong fire was kindled/flare up suddenly."
segments.s137-g41: چه گویند: 'چه' is the interrogative 'what' placed before the verb for emphasis; 'گویند' is an archaic 3rd-person plural present/formal narrative 'they say'. 'گردان' (troop, host) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order. The first 'که' begins a subordinate/relative clause describing the horse; the second 'که' is a relative marker linking 'اسپش' ('his steed/horse') to the following verb phrase. The final word here 'برد' would be expected as a verb modifying 'اسپش' (e.g. 'which Tahmētan rode/led'), but the supplied sense list does not include that verbal sense.
segments.s137-g42: تهمتن is the proper name (Tahmētan) and is the subject of the following verbs. بدین سان is the idiom 'in this manner' (ب = to/into or prefix, دین+سان = 'this way'); بخفت is the past narrative form of خفتن ('he fell/was humbled/lay down') and بمرد is the past of مردن ('he died'); the two verbs are coordinated by و. The sequence gives a simple past narrative: 'Tahmētan thus fell and died.'
segments.s137-g43: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. رفت here functions as the verbal complement of the modal باید ('must'); in classical/poetic Persian the bare past-stem is often used after باید to express necessity ('must go'). The preposition به introduces the destination/state ('into'), and بیچارگی is the noun 'misery/helplessness' serving as that destination/state.
segments.s137-g44: سپردن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to consign/entrust' and here complements the previous modal notion (what he must do). به غم دل = 'to the grief of the heart' (به + noun phrase), where غم is 'sorrow' and دل 'heart'. به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'at once/suddenly' modifying the action.
segments.s137-g45: کنون 'now' begins the clause; بست is the verb 'to bind/gird' (here used with objects 'سلیح و کمر' — weapons and belt). As in previous lines the modal باید ('must') governs the action; the verb and modal appear in inverted poetic order ('کنون بست باید'). سلیح و کمر are the coordinated direct objects (arms and girdle).
segments.s137-g46: به جایی = 'to/at some place' (به + جایی 'somewhere'). نشانش is the pronoun-attached noun 'نشان' + -ش (his/it); in context 'نشان' functions idiomatically as 'a seat/place/position for him' (an archaic sense not listed in the provided senses). بیابم is the 1st-singular present/subjunctive/optative form 'I may/find' or 'I find', and مگر here means 'unless' — together: 'unless I find some place for him.'
segments.s137-g47: همی رفت is a progressive/narrative 'he kept going' or 'he went on', where همی marks continuous or iterative aspect. زین سان is the idiom 'in this way' (زین here functioning as 'this' in the compound), and پر اندوه و رنج means 'full of sorrow and hardship' (پر = full (of), followed by the nouns اندوه and رنج). Poetic order places adverbial/qualifying material after the verb.
segments.s137-g48: This is a balanced parallel: 'تن اندر عنا' = 'the body in toil' and 'دل اندر شکنج' = 'the heart in torment.' اندر is the archaic/prepositional 'in/within', and the two noun phrases are contrasted/coordinated with و. The structure emphasizes bodily toil and mental anguish as two separate sufferings.
segments.s137-g17: چو ... شد is a temporal clause marker: چو (here 'when') + compound verb شد 'became/was' together mean 'when ... happened'. آتش پراگنده شد is 'the fire became scattered / the fire spread'. پیلتن (a proper name/place) follows that clause as the topic for the main clause that continues in the next segment.
segments.s137-g18: This is the main clause: درختی (an indefinite noun 'a tree') is the subject and بجست is the past tense 'sprang, leapt' (literary / heroic past). از در بابزن is a locational/agentive phrase 'from the gate / from the gate‑keeper' with از marking origin. Word order is topic (پیلتن in the previous segment) → subject → verb → source phrase.
segments.s137-g19: یکی here is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one', placed before the noun phrase. نره qualifies it as 'male (of an animal)'. گوری is a name/ethnic/animal-word in the text (here the specific creature attacked); بزد is the past tense 'he struck / smote' and بر + درخت means 'upon the tree' (بر as preposition + object).
segments.s137-g20: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/which', here giving the reason or circumstance. در چنگ او is a genitive/locative expression 'in his claw' (در = in, چنگ = claw/talon), پر مرغی is 'a bird's feather' (پر = feather, مرغی = avian / little bird), and نسخت is a poetic/past form used here meaning 'was stuck / fixed' (the listed dictionary senses don't match exactly; grammatically this clause explains why he struck the tree).
segments.s137-g21: چو + بریان شد is a temporal clause 'when it was roasted' (بریان = roasted; شد marks the past/result). از هم بکند و بخورد is a coordinated action: از هم بکند literally 'he tore apart from one another' (هم here indicates mutual/combined parts) and بخورد is the past 'he ate' (literary past form). The verb forms are narrative pasts typical of epic style.
segments.s137-g22: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from', مغز استخوانش = 'the marrow of its bones' (مغز = marrow/brain, استخوانش = its bone), برآورد گرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'he brought out / scraped out / produced' in epic diction (برآورد + گرد is a compound result/continuousive expression). The exact nuance of برآورد here is literary 'drew forth'.
segments.s137-g23: بخفت is the past tense 'he slept' (classical/poetic past). و links the coordinated predicate برآسود 'he rested / was relieved' (برآسود is a stative past). از روزگار uses از with روزگار meaning 'the fortunes / affairs of life' (here 'from the troubles of fate'), so the clause means he slept and was eased from worldly cares.
segments.s137-g24: This is a nominal adjunct describing the scene: چمان و چران are paired nouns meaning grazing/browsing actions (poetic diction) and modify رخش 'his steed' (رخش = steed). در مرغزار is a locative 'in the meadow/pasture'. Word order places the actions first (poetic parallelism) then the possessed noun and location.
segments.s137-g33: چو is a temporal particle here meaning “when.” بیدار شد is a compound in which شد (past of شدن) functions as the main past tense verbal element, so بیدار شد = “awoke / became awake.” The name رستم follows the verb (poetic inversion) and از خواب خوش is the prepositional phrase “from a pleasant sleep” with the adjective خوش placed after خواب in the classical order.
segments.s137-g34: به کار آمدش is an idiomatic verbal phrase: به کار آمد = “came to use / proved useful,” with the enclitic -ش = “to him.” The noun phrase باره دستکش follows and identifies what proved useful (دستکش = “glove”); باره here is an occasional/temporal/pointing word (“on that occasion” / “then”) linking the noun to the verb.
segments.s137-g35: بدان here functions as a compound prepositional phrase equivalent to “to/into that” (classical به + آن); مرغزار اندرون = “the meadow’s interior / the inner part of the meadow” where the adjective/place-word اندرون follows the noun. بنگرید is the classical narrative/look verb (bn- prefix form), used here as the main past narrative verb “he looked” placed at the end.
segments.s137-g36: ز هر سو = “from every direction / on every side.” همی is a classical narrative particle appearing before the object/verb (here functioning as an emphatic or continuous particle), and بارگی را ندید is the object (بارگی “foliage/leaf”) with the definite object marker را followed by the negative past ندید “he did not see.”
segments.s137-g37: غمی گشت is literally “he became sorrow” = “he grew grief-stricken,” with گشت (from گشتن) meaning “became/turned (into).” چون introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/circumstance (“because/when”), and بارگی را نیافت is the object with را and the negative past نیافت “he did not find.”
segments.s137-g38: سراسیمه is an adverb meaning “frantically / distraughtly” modifying the verb that follows. سوی سمنگان = “toward Samangan” (سوی = toward, سمنگان = place-name), and شتافت is the past tense of شتافتن “to hasten / rush,” so the clause means “he hurried off toward Samangan.”
segments.s137-g39: همی گفت is a habitual/continuative narrative construction meaning “he kept saying” or “he said repeatedly.” کاکنون (= کا اکنون) = “now” or “at this moment.” پیاده دوان is an adverbial phrase “on foot, running” (پیاده = on foot; دوان = running/hurrying) that sets the manner for the action expressed in the speech that follows.
segments.s137-g40: This is direct speech. کجا = “where.” پویم here is a first‑person verbal form meaning “shall I go/run?” (classical subjunctive/optative sense – the provided lemma list has only the noun sense, so the correct verbal sense is represented by -1). از introduces the source/reason “from” and ننگ تیره‌روان = “the shame of (his) dark/ill‑fated state,” where the adjective تیره‌روان follows and qualifies ننگ.

sentences.s137-s13: A band of Turkish horsemen, seven or eight strong, passed over that hunting plain.
sentences.s137-s14: They saw a horse in the meadow and turned about along the stream's bank.
sentences.s137-s15: When they found my Rakhsh on the plain, they hurried to tie him up.
sentences.s137-s16: They seized [him] and swiftly carried [him] to the city; then each one leapt from his steed toward (i.e., for) ...
sentences.s137-s1: From a dehqān's words I relate a tale taken from ancient report.
sentences.s137-s2: From the mobed I thus received this remembrance: that Rostam, one morning at dawn...
sentences.s137-s3: A grievous sorrow set his heart on the hunt; he girded his waist and filled his quiver with arrows.
sentences.s137-s4: When he set his face toward the border of Turan, like a lion, watchful and seeking prey —
sentences.s137-s5: When he came near the border of Turan, he saw the countryside entirely full of graves.
sentences.s137-s6: His face flushed like a flower; the crown-giver laughed, and his steed uprooted from its place.
sentences.s137-s7: With bow and arrow and with mace and lasso they cast onto the plain a few heads of game.
sentences.s137-s8: From chaff and from thorn and bough of the tree, a single fierce fire suddenly kindled.
sentences.s137-s21: What will the host say — that the horse which Tahmētan had ridden thus stumbled, and he fell and died?
sentences.s137-s22: Now he must go into misery, consigning his heart to sorrow in an instant.
sentences.s137-s23: Now I must gird on weapons and belt and find some place for him, unless (I cannot).
sentences.s137-s24: Thus he went on, full of grief and suffering, his body in toil and his heart in torment.
sentences.s137-s9: When the fire spread, in Piltan a tree sprang up from the gate‑keeper.
sentences.s137-s10: A certain male gori struck at the tree, for in his claw a bird's feather was fastened.
sentences.s137-s11: When it had been roasted, he tore it apart and ate it; from the marrow of its bones he scraped out the core.
sentences.s137-s12: He slept and found repose from the fortunes of life, while his steed grazed and browsed in the meadow.
sentences.s137-s17: When Rostam awoke from a pleasant sleep, a glove came in handy for him.
sentences.s137-s18: He looked into that meadow, and from every side he saw no leaf.
sentences.s137-s19: He was grieved when he could not find the leaf; distraught, he rushed toward Samangan.
sentences.s137-s20: He kept saying, 'Where now shall I run on foot to escape the shame of my dark‑fated lot?'
